Acknowledgements We would like to thank the following organizations and representatives for their support: 3.1 Buffalo City College – Aphinda Danisa, Liaison Officer 3.2 Walter Sisulu University – Sisipho Qwenga, (Marketing Department) 3.3 Buffalo Metro Inner City High School – Mr. M Mamatu (Principal), Mr. Pilliso (HOD Of Maths & Technology) 4 4 4. Introduction AlumniBridge is a web-based platform designed to connect alumni and students t

Principal), Mr. Pilliso (HOD Of Maths & Technology) 4 4 4. Introduction AlumniBridge is a web-based platform designed to connect alumni and students through real- world opportunities. The app enables alumni to post career openings, internships, or other professional opportunities directly to students within their institution. Students can explore these opportunities and apply for those that match their interests. By centralizing this interaction, AlumniBridge streamlines the process of opportunity sharing and application tracking, making it easier for students to discover alumni-supported pathways to career growth. Beyond connecting users, AlumniBridge introduces a results-driven feature that allows students to report the outcomes of their applications — whether successful or not. This feedback loop helps the system generate automatic monthly reports that summarize the success rate of op

applications — whether successful or not. This feedback loop helps the system generate automatic monthly reports that summarize the success rate of opportunities, providing valuable insights into student engagement and employment outcomes. The motivation behind this development is to create a transparent, data-informed ecosystem that fosters collaboration between alumni and students. 5 5 5. Requirements and Their Limitations 5.1 Requirements 5.2 Limitations The platform's effectiveness is fundamentally tied to active alumni involvement, as limited engagement could significantly reduce the number of available opportunities for students. Furthermore, the accuracy of its real-time data is contingent on students consistently and manually updating their application outcomes, a process that can lead to incomplete or unreliable reports. Since all user interactions require internet connectivity,

ng their application outcomes, a process that can lead to incomplete or unreliable reports. Since all user interactions require internet connectivity, the system cannot be used offline, potentially limiting accessibility. Integration with external job boards or APIs may also be restricted by institutional data policies or technical hurdles. Consequently, the automated reporting, which relies on this user-input data, may lack precision in its analytics of success rates. It is also important to note that the current system is designed for alumni-to-student interactions and does not yet incorporate functionality for employers or recruiters. 6 6 6. Use-Case Diagram and Assessment of Its Quality 6.1 Use-Case Diagram Overview The Alumni Bridge system is designed around two primary user groups: Alumni, who post job opportunities, and update their profiles, and Students, who search and apply for

stem is designed around two primary user groups: Alumni, who post job opportunities, and update their profiles, and Students, who search and apply for these opportunities and provide feedback on their outcomes. This interaction is captured in a use-case diagram featuring these two actors placed on opposite sides of a system boundary box labeled "Alumni Bridge." Inside this boundary, the core functions are represented as distinct use cases; for Alumni, these include posting opportunities, updating their profile and managing job visibility, while for Students, the key use cases are browsing opportunities, tracking their status, and providing outcome feedback. Association lines directly link each actor to their respective set of functions, visually mapping the interactions, such as connecting the student to "Apply for Opportunity" or the Alumni to "Post Opportunities." 6.2 Assessment of Use

ually mapping the interactions, such as connecting the student to "Apply for Opportunity" or the Alumni to "Post Opportunities." 6.2 Assessment of Use-Case Quality The Alumni Bridge use-case diagram demonstrates a high degree of clarity by clearly defining actor roles, employing concise and singular use cases, and establishing well-defined system boundaries that logically group all functionalities. Its comprehensiveness is evident in its coverage of all key requirements — from job posting and application to tracking and feedback — while also incorporating extended features like reporting to capture critical operations beyond the primary ones. The diagram is both correct and relevant, as each use case corresponds directly to an implemented system feature, the relationships between actors and functions are accurately 7 7 depicted, and the overall scope effectively reflects the core objecti

ystem feature, the relationships between actors and functions are accurately 7 7 depicted, and the overall scope effectively reflects the core objective of facilitating alumni-student engagement. For future improvement, the diagram could be enhanced by adding sub-use cases for notifications and administrative moderation to increase completeness, and the use of color coding for different actors could further improve its readability in presentations. 8 8 7. They can apply to opportunities directly through the app and track the status of each application (e.g., clicked, applied, pending). After the application process, students c

unities directly through the app and track the status of each application (e.g., clicked, applied, pending). After the application process, students can provide feedback indicating whether their pursuit was successful or unsuccessful. Implementation: 8.2.1 Applications are logged in the APPLICATION_TRACKING table, linking students ( PERSONS.PID ) to opportunities ( OPPORTUNITIES.OID ). 8.2.2 Status updates and outcomes are tracked with fields like STATUS , REMINDER_SENT , and OUTCOME . 8.2.3 The system generates alerts ( ALERTS table) to notify students of important events or updates in their application process. 8.2.4 Views like LODSTUDENT and LOGGEDALUMNI streamline data retrieval for personalized dashboards. 8.3 Automatic Reporting Description: AlumniBridge generates automated monthly reports summarizing the success rates of student applications. These reports show the number of oppor

iption: AlumniBridge generates automated monthly reports summarizing the success rates of student applications. These reports show the number of opportunities applied for, completed successfully, or unsuccessful, helping both alumni and administrators monitor engagement and effectiveness. Implementation: 8.3.1 The reporting engine queries APPLICATION_TRACKING , joining with OPPORTUNITIES and FULLJOBDETAILS to generate meaningful metrics. 8.3.2 Aggregated data is displayed in the app’s dashboard for both alumni and administrators. 8.3.3 Alerts and notifications are triggered based on the report data, keeping users informed of critical updates. 8.4 Additional Features 8.4.1 Profile Management: Alumni and students can update their profiles, including educational background, certificates ( CERTIFICATE table), and institutional affiliations ( INSTITUTION table). 12 12 8.4.2 Secure Access and

cluding educational background, certificates ( CERTIFICATE table), and institutional affiliations ( INSTITUTION table). 12 12 8.4.2 Secure Access and Personalization: Views such as LOGGEDINST , LOGEDU , and CERTIFICATE1 provide user-specific access to relevant data using APEX_UTIL.GET_USERNAME , ensuring personalized and secure information retrieval. 13 13 9. Unresolved Challenges and Recommendations for Improvement 9.1 Challenges 9.1.1 User Engagement: Encouraging consistent participation from alumni remains a challenge. Without regular opportunity postings, student activity on the platform may decline. 9.1.2 Data Accuracy: The system relies on students manually updating their application outcomes (successful or unsuccessful). This dependency can lead to incomplete or inaccurate monthly reports. 9.1.3 Automated Analytics: While the platform generates monthly reports, fine-tuning the acc

ncy can lead to incomplete or inaccurate monthly reports. 9.1.3 Automated Analytics: While the platform generates monthly reports, fine-tuning the accuracy and visualization of these analytics is still an ongoing task. 9.1.4 Scalability and Performance: As the number of users and opportunities increases, the system may require optimization to maintain performance and quick data retrieval. 9.1.5 Integration with External Systems: Connecting AlumniBridge with third-party job boards, institutional databases, or alumni management systems presents technical and permission-related constraints. 9.1.6 User Interface Consistency: Ensuring the interface looks and performs consistently across different devices and browsers remains a minor design challenge. 9.2 Recommendations 9.2.1 Implement Push Notifications and Reminders: Introduce automated notifications to remind alumni to post new opportuniti

enge. 9.2 Recommendations 9.2.1 Implement Push Notifications and Reminders: Introduce automated notifications to remind alumni to post new opportunities and prompt students to update their application statuses. 9.2.2 Add Verification for Report Updates: Include a validation system or confirmation mechanism to ensure that outcome reports are genuine and complete. 9.2.3 Enhance Data Visualization: Improve monthly reports with interactive charts and filters to give administrators clearer insights into success rates and trends. 9.2.4 Optimization for Scalability: Upgrade the database structure and caching mechanisms to handle larger datasets efficiently as the platform grows. 9.2.5 Enable External Integrations: In future versions, allow integration with job boards, or institutional APIs to broaden the range of opportunities available to students. 9.2.6 Improve Mobile Experience: Refine the m

tion with job boards, or institutional APIs to broaden the range of opportunities available to students. 9.2.6 Improve Mobile Experience: Refine the mobile layout and responsiveness for smoother navigation and better accessibility on all devices. 14 14 10. Conclusion The Alumni Bridge project successfully delivers a dynamic platform that connects alumni and students through meaningful opportunities. By allowing alumni to post available positions and enabling students to apply directly within the system, the application bridges the gap between academic learning and professional growth. The inclusion of outcome tracking — where students can mark their application results — adds a valuable layer of transparency and accountability, helping both students and institutions assess real employment progress. The automatic generation of monthly success reports further enhances the system’s usefulne

students and institutions assess real employment progress. The automatic generation of monthly success reports further enhances the system’s usefulness, providing institutions with data-driven insights into student engagement and opportunity effectiveness. Although some challenges remain, such as ensuring active participation from alumni and improving data accuracy, Alumni Bridge sets up a strong foundation for fostering collaboration, and career advancement within the academic community. With continued development and user engagement, Alumni Bridge has the potential to become a long-term digital bridge that strengthens institutional relationships and supports students in achieving real-world success. 11. Annexures Presentation of Cooperating Organizations The AlumniBridge project was developed in collaboration with Walter Sisulu University as part of an initiative to strengthen connect

ing Organizations The AlumniBridge project was developed in collaboration with Walter Sisulu University as part of an initiative to strengthen connections between students and alumni. The institution provided access to academic data, user requirements, and platform testing environments to ensure that the system aligns with real-world university needs. The cooperating organization —represented by the university’s Marketing Department , the Buffalo City College’s Marketing Department and the BMIC High School’s Maths and Technology Department — played a key role in identifying functional requirements, validating system objectives, and providing feedback during testing phases. Their participation ensured that AlumniBridge addresses institutional goals such as enhancing graduate employability, encouraging alumni involvement, and promoting mentorship opportunities.

The sequential circuit makes use of combinational circuits. • Combinational circuits implement the essential functions of a digital computer. However, except for the special case of ROM, they provide no memory or state information, elements also essential to the operation of a digital computer. © 20 24 19 • For the latter purposes, a more complex form of digital logic circuit is used: the sequential circuit. • The current output of a sequential circuit depends not only on the current input, but also on the past history of input

sed: the sequential circuit. • The current output of a sequential circuit depends not only on the current input, but also on the past history of inputs. • Another and generally more useful way to view it is that the current output of a sequential circuit depends on the current input and the current state of that circuit. • Sequential logic circuits are able to take into account their previous input state as well as those actually present, a sort of “before” and “after” effect is involved with sequential circuits. • In other words, the output state of a “sequential logic circuit” is a function of the following three states, the “ present input ”, the “ past input ” and/or the “ past output ”. • Sequential Logic circuits remember these conditions and stay fixed in their current state until the next clock signal changes one of the states, giving sequential logic circuits “Memory”. • Sequent

ions and stay fixed in their current state until the next clock signal changes one of the states, giving sequential logic circuits “Memory”. • Sequential logic circuits are generally termed as two state or Bistable devices which can have their output or outputs set in one of two basic states, a logic level “1” or a logic level “0” and will remain “ latched” indefinitely in this current state or condition until some other input trigger pulse or signal is applied which will cause the bistable to change its state once again. • The word “Sequential” means that things happen in a “sequence”, one after another an d in Sequential Logic circuits. T he actual clock signal determines when things will happen next. • Simple sequential logic circuits can be constructed from standard Bistable circuits such as: Flip - flops, Latches and Counters and which themselves can be made by simply connecting tog

n be constructed from standard Bistable circuits such as: Flip - flops, Latches and Counters and which themselves can be made by simply connecting together universal NAND Gates and/or NOR Gates in a particular combinational way to produce the required sequential circuit. • As standard logic gates are the building blocks of combinational circuits, bistable latches and flip - flops are the basic building blocks of sequential logic circuits. Sequential logic circuits can be constructed to produce either simple edge - triggered flip - flops or more complex sequential circuits such as storage registers, shift registers, memory devices or counters. Either way sequential logic circuits can be divided into the following three main categories: o Event Driven – asynchronous circuits that change state immediately when enabled. o Clock Driven – synchronous circuits that are synchronized to a specifi

nt Driven – asynchronous circuits that change state immediately when enabled. o Clock Driven – synchronous circuits that are synchronized to a specific clock signal. o Pulse Driven – which is a combination of the two that responds to triggering pulses. © 20 24 20 • Examples of sequential circuits includes: o Latch : a 1 - bit memory element o Register: m latches in a row and is able to store an m - bit word o Shift register: special - purpose register that moves the bits of the word left or right o Counter: special - purpose register that when triggered (i.e. clocked) increases its contents by 1 o State machines: moves from one state to another each time it is triggered • YOU ARE GOING TO LOOK AT A FEW SEQUENTIAL CIRCUITS AS AN ASSIGNMENT. 4.5 B OOLEAN A LGEBRA • Expressions that involve AND, OR, NOT, XOR are called Boolean expressions. • If a Boolean expression is very complicated, requ
